headline: Oregon's passing attack overwhelms USC in lopsided Pac clash
body: Brock Thomas had the kind of afternoon that makes defensive coordinators lose sleep. The Oregon quarterback threaded passes with precision across a vulnerable USC secondary, completing 25 of 37 attempts for 425 yards and two touchdowns in a 47-26 rout that was never truly competitive after the opening quarter. Thomas's composure in the pocket and willingness to let plays develop downfield exposed gaps in USC's coverage scheme that the Trojan defense simply could not adjust to over four quarters.

The disparity in total offense told the real story: Oregon accumulated 556 yards to USC's meager 258, a gap that reflected fundamental differences in execution and game plan. While Oregon's ground game remained pedestrian with just 135 rushing yards, the passing attack more than compensated. Wide receiver Perry's five catches for 121 yards and consistent intermediate routes kept chains moving and kept USC's defense on its heels. USC quarterback Moss threw for 226 yards and three touchdowns, respectable numbers on the surface, but he spent much of the day operating in catch-up mode, forced into a rhythm that favored quick passes over systematic drives.

Oregon's turnover discipline proved crucial where it mattered most. Zero turnovers allowed the Ducks to control tempo and field position without self-inflicted wounds. USC's one fumble, meanwhile, punctuated an afternoon of missed opportunities and mounting frustration. The coaching staff's ability to keep Thomas upright and running a balanced attack—even when it didn't always show in rushing statistics—gave Oregon the structural advantage that turned a conference matchup into a statement victory.
turningPoint: In the second quarter, Thomas connected with Perry on a 34-yard gain that set up a touchdown and effectively put USC into an unrecoverable deficit from which their defense never recovered.

unsungHero.name: Perry
unsungHero.why: Thomas gets the spotlight for his quarterback excellence, but Perry's five receptions for 121 yards provided the reliable target option that allowed Oregon to attack vertically and control the game's tempo without needing the ground game.
